card.title: Modus Ponens: Logic's Bedrock
card.teaser: The world's shortest, oldest, and most unbreakable argument — taught by the Stoics, trusted by every mathematician alive.
card.coverImagePrompt: A marble Stoic philosopher's bust in a sunlit colonnade, with a soft chalkboard overlay of 'P → Q' floating beside him, painterly, classical aesthetic

lessons.title: The three-line engine of proof
lessons.body: If it's raining, the street is wet. It's raining. Therefore, the street is wet. Trivial? Sure. But this tiny pattern — called modus ponens — is the engine underneath nearly every mathematical proof, legal argument, and line of code ever written. You've used it a thousand times today without noticing.

visual.imagePrompt: A rain-slicked cobblestone street reflecting streetlamp light, droplets visible, subtle poetic mood, cinematic

lessons.title: The Stoic formula
lessons.body: Ancient Stoic logicians like Chrysippus, writing around 250 BCE, formalized it as: 'If the first, then the second. The first. Therefore, the second.' In modern notation: P → Q. P. ∴ Q. The Latin name, 'modus ponens,' means 'the way that affirms' — because you affirm the 'if' part to unlock the 'then' part.

keyTerms.definition: The inference rule: from (P → Q) and P, conclude Q
An 'if P then Q' statement, the backbone of most logical proofs

visual.imagePrompt: A chalkboard showing 'P → Q, P ⊢ Q' in elegant handwritten mathematical notation, lit by a desk lamp

lessons.title: The imposter that looks the same
lessons.body: Watch out for modus ponens' evil twin: affirming the consequent. 'If it rains, the street is wet. The street is wet. Therefore it rained.' Wrong! The street might be wet because a truck sprayed it. Modus ponens goes from cause to effect. Affirming the consequent illegally runs the argument backwards — a fallacy that fools even sharp thinkers.

visual.imagePrompt: A street wet from a water truck instead of rain, the truck visible in the distance, clear blue sky overhead — gentle visual irony

lessons.title: Why this tiny rule matters
lessons.body: Every formal system — Euclidean geometry, programming languages, mathematical proofs, court verdicts — relies on modus ponens as a trusted move. It's so obvious it feels empty, yet it's what allows long chains of reasoning to stay rigorous. Philosopher Lewis Carroll once wrote a puzzle suggesting you can't even talk someone into accepting modus ponens without using modus ponens. It's logic's unshakable floor.

quiz.question: Which is a valid modus ponens argument?
options: If P then Q. Q. Therefore P.
If P then Q. Not P. Therefore not Q.
If P then Q. P. Therefore Q.
P or Q. Not P. Therefore Q.
quiz.explanation: Modus ponens: affirm the antecedent (P), and you may conclude the consequent (Q).

quiz.question: 'The street is wet, so it must have rained' is an example of:
quiz.explanation: Running 'if P then Q' backwards — from Q to P — is the fallacy of affirming the consequent. The cause may not be what you think.

quiz.question: Who is most associated with the earliest systematic treatment of modus ponens?
quiz.explanation: Chrysippus and the Stoic logicians developed propositional logic (including modus ponens) around 250 BCE — distinct from Aristotle's syllogistic logic.

sources.title: What the Tortoise Said to Achilles
